title: Modernizing a Migrated Monolith
description:
## Reducing Ops Overhead After a Lift-and-Shift

A company rehosted a monolithic application onto a large Amazon EC2 instance. In production they observe two pain points: a few components experience **bursty, unpredictable traffic** (driving them to oversize the whole instance for peak), while the rest of the app is steady. The team also spends significant time **patching and operating the servers and a self-managed database**. They want to **reduce operational overhead** and **scale the bursty components independently** of the rest. Which modernization approach is BEST?

options.text: Refactor the bursty components into AWS Lambda functions (or containers on ECS/Fargate) that scale independently, and move the self-managed database to a managed service such as Amazon RDS/Aurora or DynamoDB
Keep the monolith as-is and move it to a larger EC2 instance type to absorb the bursts
Rehost the same monolith again onto an EC2 Auto Scaling group of identical large instances
Leave the architecture unchanged and add more read replicas to the self-managed database

explanation:
The goals are **independent scaling of bursty components** and **lower operational overhead**. The right post-migration move is to **refactor**: peel the bursty components out of the monolith into **Lambda** (or containers on **ECS/Fargate**) so they scale on demand without oversizing the whole app, and shift the self-managed database to a **managed data store** (RDS/Aurora or DynamoDB) so AWS handles patching, backups, and HA. This decouples scaling and cuts the ops burden directly.

(b) A **bigger instance** is vertical scaling of the entire monolith — you still pay for peak across all components, you still can't scale the bursty parts independently, and the operational burden (patching, self-managed DB) remains. (c) **Auto Scaling identical monolith instances** scales the *whole* application as a unit, not the bursty components individually, and replicating a large monolith is wasteful; it also leaves the DB self-managed. (d) **Read replicas** address read load on the DB but do nothing for component-level scaling or the operational overhead of self-managing the database and servers. Only refactoring the bursty pieces to serverless/containers plus managed data stores meets both objectives.

hints.title: Independent scaling
hints.body: Scaling a whole monolith (bigger box or more identical boxes) is not the same as scaling individual components — that requires decoupling.

hints.title: Operational overhead
hints.body: Managed services (Lambda, Fargate, RDS/Aurora, DynamoDB) remove patching/backup/HA toil that self-managed EC2 + DB carries.
